case_name: Alachlor removal from agricultural well water for municipal drinking supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for alachlor-contaminated well water intended for drinking water production under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town uses a deep well influenced by surrounding row-crop agriculture and has detected alachlor in the raw water. The utility needs a reliable treatment upgrade to produce drinking water using established, practical technologies. Operators can manage routine media changeout and standard disinfection, but the system does not want high-energy or highly specialized treatment.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for alachlor and provide stable, acceptable finished water quality for municipal distribution.
secondary_goals: use proven technology for pesticide removal

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: remove particulate matter and protect downstream adsorptive media from fouling
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb alachlor and related hydrophobic organic micropollutants

applicability_rationale: alachlor is a synthetic organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach in drinking water treatment
the well water has relatively low turbidity and modest DOC, making GAC practical and effective with reasonable bed life
the utility prefers conventional, proven treatment with moderate energy demand
GAC systems are commonly applied for taste, odor, and pesticide control and fit municipal operation capabilities
potential_risks: natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten carbon run length
breakthrough monitoring is needed because pesticide occurrence may be seasonal
spent carbon replacement or reactivation planning is required

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: reduce incoming solids and improve downstream clarification or filtration performance
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: provide adsorptive removal of alachlor from the water phase
core_unit_functions.contact_tank: allow adsorption contact time before solids separation
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove PAC-associated solids and polish finished water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized conventional option for episodic or seasonal synthetic organic contaminant control
the contamination pattern may vary with agricultural conditions, so dose adjustment can offer operational flexibility
this approach can be implemented where a utility prefers conventional chemical-feed style operation
it avoids the higher energy and concentrate burdens of membrane systems
potential_risks: PAC performance depends strongly on dose, mixing, contact time, and competing DOC
more solids handling is required than with fixed-bed GAC
short-term upset conditions can reduce removal consistency if dosing is not adjusted properly

core_unit_functions.aeration_or_pre_filtration: prepare water and reduce factors that interfere with oxidation or downstream media
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation: oxidize alachlor and transform it into more degradable or removable compounds
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon_or_gac: adsorb residual organics and polish oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can treat pesticide contaminants when strong destruction or polishing is desired
pairing oxidation with carbon polishing addresses both parent compound and byproduct concerns
the approach is technically feasible for low-turbidity well water
it is ranked below simple carbon adsorption because the case emphasizes conventional operation and moderate energy use
potential_risks: oxidation byproducts must be assessed rather than assuming complete mineralization
process control is more complex than straightforward adsorption
energy and chemical requirements are higher than for carbon-only treatment

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical rejection of alachlor and other dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.post_stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically capable of reducing many organic micropollutants including pesticides
it may be selected if broader dissolved contaminant control is also required in the future
the low-turbidity well water is generally favorable for membrane pretreatment requirements
potential_risks: concentrate management is a significant drawback for inland well-water systems
high-pressure membranes add energy demand and operator burden beyond what is needed for a single primary pesticide issue
membrane treatment may be unnecessarily complex for this otherwise straightforward case

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: attempt to transfer volatile contaminants from water to air
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish water and remove incidental particulate matter

applicability_rationale: included as a lower-ranked comparator because it is a known process for some organic contaminants
it may be considered in treatment screening when contaminant properties are uncertain or mixed volatile organics are present
potential_risks: air stripping is generally not a preferred treatment for alachlor because adsorption-based methods are better supported for this pesticide
off-gas control may be needed if volatile co-contaminants exist
selection of this process could lead to underperformance for the stated target contaminant

must_reflect: proven technology preference for pesticide removal
moderate operational capability with conventional utility staffing
avoidance of unnecessarily high-energy or high-residuals solutions

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for alachlor removal
how DOC or natural organic matter can affect adsorption performance
why membrane options rank lower under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints

should_mention: byproduct or residual management considerations for advanced oxidation or membranes
